title: Scaling Web Application
description: A step-by-step walkthrough of evolving a simple web application from a single-server setup to a multi-region, sharded, message-queue-driven architecture that supports millions of users.

nodes.description: Browser: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

functional: Serve a web application over www.mysite.com (HTML/CSS/JS) and a JSON API over api.mysite.com.
Resolve domain names via DNS and route requests to the correct backend.
Read and write user data persistently across requests and process restarts.
Serve static assets (images, JS, CSS, video) globally with low latency.
Run asynchronous background work (e.g. photo processing, email sending) without blocking the request path.
Operate across multiple geographic regions with failover on a regional outage.
nonFunctional: Scale from a single user to millions without re-architecting at every order of magnitude.
High availability — no single point of failure at the web, cache, or data tier.
Low latency for both dynamic responses and static content.
Horizontal scalability — add capacity by adding servers, not by buying bigger ones.
Durable storage — committed data must survive node and data-center failures.
Observable — logs, metrics, and automation are first-class once the system is large.

capacity.label: Users
capacity.note: The whole point of the chapter: the same design pattern must stretch across this range.

capacity.label: Read/write ratio
capacity.note: Most workloads have far more reads than writes — drives the cache + replica decisions.

capacity.label: Static content share
capacity.note: JS/CSS/images/video dominate egress; CDN absorbs most of it.

capacity.label: Cache working set
capacity.note: Sized to hold the hot subset; LRU eviction handles the long tail.

capacity.label: Regional outages
capacity.note: Two+ data centers with geoDNS failover.

api.description: Fetch a user record as JSON. Representative of the API surface served on api.mysite.com.
Serve the web application HTML for www.mysite.com. Static assets are linked from CDN URLs.
api.response: HTTP 200 text/html with links to CDN-hosted JS/CSS/images.

api.description: Upload a photo for asynchronous processing (crop, sharpen, blur). The request returns immediately while a worker handles the heavy lifting.
api.request: multipart/form-data with the image file and processing options

api.description: Poll the status of a queued photo-processing job.

messages.label: SELECT status FROM jobs WHERE id=?

dataModel.name: users
dataModel.note: Primary user record. Sharded by user_id hash once a single DB instance no longer fits.

dataModel.name: sessions
dataModel.note: Session data moved out of the web tier so any server can serve any request (stateless tier).

fields.name: data
fields.note: Serialized session blob; could also live in Redis/NoSQL

fields.name: url
fields.note: Points at the processed asset on CDN

dataModel.name: jobs
dataModel.note: Async work tracked via the message queue. Workers update status as they progress.

patterns.name: Load balancing
patterns.what: Put a load balancer in front of a pool of identical servers; it spreads requests and routes around unhealthy instances via health checks.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever one server is both a single point of failure and a throughput ceiling, and you want to scale a tier horizontally.

patterns.name: Replication
patterns.what: Keep copies of the data on multiple nodes; route writes to a primary and reads to replicas to spread read load and survive a node loss.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy workloads, or when the data tier is a single point of failure.

patterns.name: Cache-aside
patterns.what: Read from the cache first; on a miss, read the source of truth and backfill the cache so later reads are fast.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy access with a hot subset of keys, where a slightly stale read is acceptable.

patterns.name: Edge caching (CDN)
patterns.what: Serve cacheable static responses from points of presence close to users instead of from the origin.
patterns.whenToUse: Geographically distributed read traffic for cacheable content (static assets, media).
patterns.group: 6. Add a CDN for Static Content

patterns.name: Stateless web tier
patterns.what: Keep no per-user state in web-server memory; push session state to a shared store (or a signed token) so any server can handle any request.
patterns.whenToUse: When you need autoscaling, rolling deploys, and failover without losing user context.

patterns.name: Sticky sessions (session affinity)
patterns.what: Pin each user to one web server via load-balancer affinity so their in-memory session stays reachable, avoiding a shared session store.
patterns.whenToUse: A quick way to scale out a session-bearing app, but it breaks autoscaling and graceful deploys; prefer an external session store or stateless tokens.

patterns.name: Work queue
patterns.what: Hand slow or bursty work to a queue that worker processes consume asynchronously, decoupling request latency from background processing.
patterns.whenToUse: Long-running, retryable, or spiky tasks (email, image processing, exports) that needn't block the request.

patterns.name: Horizontal sharding (partitioning)
patterns.what: Split data across nodes by a partition key so no single node holds the whole dataset or its full write volume.
patterns.whenToUse: When data size or write throughput exceeds what one (replicated) database node can serve.

functional.requirement: Serve a web application and JSON API under different domains.
functional.how: DNS resolves www.mysite.com and api.mysite.com to the same edge entry point; the web tier handles both surfaces. Once the LB is added, both domains terminate on it.
A dedicated database tier holds durable state; later, master/slave replication and sharding scale reads and storage.

functional.requirement: Serve static assets globally with low latency.
functional.how: A CDN caches JS/CSS/images close to users; web servers only serve dynamic responses.

functional.requirement: Run async background work without blocking the request path.
functional.how: Web servers publish jobs to a message queue; pools of workers consume independently and scale based on queue depth.

functional.requirement: Operate across multiple regions with failover.
functional.how: GeoDNS routes users to the nearest data center; on a regional outage, traffic is redirected entirely to the healthy DC.

nonFunctional.requirement: Scale from one user to millions.
nonFunctional.how: The architecture evolves by layering: split tiers, replicate, cache, push static to the edge, go stateless, multi-region, queue async work, and finally shard the database.
Load balancer fronts multiple web servers; DB replication + slave promotion handle DB failure; multi-DC handles regional failure; cache and CDN have replicas / managed redundancy.

nonFunctional.requirement: Low latency for dynamic and static content.
nonFunctional.how: Cache absorbs hot DB reads; CDN serves static bytes close to the user; geoDNS routes to the nearest DC.
Web tier scales out behind the LB; cache and DB shard horizontally; workers scale by adding consumers.
Master/slave replication preserves data across nodes; multi-DC replication preserves it across regions.
Logging, metrics, and automation tooling are introduced once the system is large enough that human-only operation is no longer viable.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted (HAProxy/Envoy) when you need custom routing logic, want to avoid per-rule billing, or run on-prem; choose managed when you want zero-ops health checks, autoscaling backends, and built-in DDoS/WAF and global anycast.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A global anycast load balancer (AWS Global Accelerator, GCP global Cloud Load Balancing, Azure Front Door) can route users to the nearest healthy region for you, so you may never build your own GeoDNS-plus-regional-LB tier in the multi-DC step.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted VMs/containers when you need full control of the runtime, predictable steady-state cost, or specialized hardware; choose managed (Fargate, Cloud Run, App Service, Lambda) when you want to offload patching, scaling, and capacity planning and pay per use.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Serverless or fully-managed container platforms (Lambda, Cloud Run, Fargate, Container Apps) scale instances in and out automatically, so a single beefy server in the single-server step plus a hand-built autoscaling group may be unnecessary.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted Redis/Memcached when you want to control eviction and cost and already run a cache; choose managed session stores or identity services when you want HA, failover, and backups without operating the cluster.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Signed stateless tokens (JWT) push session state to the client, which can eliminate the shared session store entirely; conversely a managed identity provider (Cognito, Identity Platform, Entra ID) can make custom server-side session handling unnecessary.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted Redis/Valkey when you want fine-grained control over data structures, persistence, and cost; choose managed caches when you want automatic failover, scaling, patching, and multi-AZ replication out of the box.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed, replicated in-memory cache (ElastiCache, MemoryDB, Memorystore, Azure Cache for Redis) absorbs read load so well that you may avoid adding database read replicas or sharding purely for read throughput in later steps.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted reverse-proxy caches (Varnish, Nginx, ATS) when you have a few PoPs or strict data-locality needs; choose a managed CDN when you want a global edge footprint, TLS termination, and DDoS protection without running edge hardware.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Serving static assets from object storage behind a CDN (S3+CloudFront, Cloud Storage+Cloud CDN, Blob+Front Door) offloads almost all static traffic from the origin, so the single-server step no longer needs to scale up just to serve images, CSS, and JS.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted PostgreSQL/MySQL when you need full control over extensions, versions, and tuning or want to avoid managed-DB premiums; choose managed RDS/Cloud SQL/Azure SQL when you want automated backups, patching, failover, and point-in-time recovery.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A distributed/auto-scaling SQL engine (Spanner, AlloyDB, Aurora, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB, TiDB) can absorb growth and rebalance data internally, so you may never hand-shard the database yourself in the sharding step.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted replication tooling (Patroni, Orchestrator, Galera) when you need control over failover policy and topology; choose managed HA (Multi-AZ, failover groups, Aurora Global Database) when you want automated failover and cross-region replicas without building the control plane.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed multi-region database offerings (Aurora Global Database, Spanner, Cosmos DB multi-region, DynamoDB Global Tables) handle cross-DC replication and failover for you, so you may not need to build custom replication and promotion logic in the multi-DC step.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose a self-hosted sharding layer (Vitess, Citus, ShardingSphere) when you must stay on a specific engine and control the shard map; choose a natively distributed store (Spanner, CockroachDB, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB) when you want automatic partitioning and rebalancing.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Picking a horizontally-scalable engine from the start (Spanner, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB) makes the manual app-level sharding, shard-routing logic, and resharding migrations of this step unnecessary.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted NoSQL (Cassandra, MongoDB, ScyllaDB) when you need control over consistency tuning and want to avoid request-unit pricing; choose managed (DynamoDB, Firestore, Cosmos DB) when you want serverless scaling, replication, and zero cluster operations.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed wide-column or document store that auto-partitions on a key (DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, Bigtable, Firestore) can serve massive scale without manual sharding, so the relational-DB sharding effort in this dataset may be sidestepped entirely for those workloads.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted object storage (MinIO, Ceph, SeaweedFS) when you need on-prem data residency or S3-compatible APIs without cloud lock-in; choose managed (S3, Cloud Storage, Blob Storage) when you want eleven-nines durability, lifecycle tiering, and effortless scale.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Durable managed object storage with event notifications (S3 events, Cloud Storage Pub/Sub notifications, Event Grid on Blob) can replace handing large payloads through the queue and can also serve as the static origin for the CDN, reducing what the message-queue step must carry.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS) when you need high-throughput streaming, replay, or want to avoid per-message pricing; choose managed (SQS/SNS, Pub/Sub, Service Bus) when you want zero-ops durability, autoscaling, and built-in dead-letter handling.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Fully managed queues with built-in retries, dead-letter queues, and fan-out (SQS+SNS, Pub/Sub, Service Bus+Event Grid) remove the need to operate Kafka/RabbitMQ clusters and to hand-build retry, backoff, and DLQ machinery in this step.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted global tooling (GeoDNS, anycast, multi-region Cockroach/Cassandra) when you span clouds or on-prem and need full control of routing and replication; choose managed global services when you want turnkey latency routing, cross-region data, and failover.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Globally-distributed managed data (Spanner, Aurora Global Database, DynamoDB Global Tables, Cosmos DB multi-region) plus an anycast global front door can deliver active-active multi-region without you building custom GeoDNS, cross-region replication pipelines, and failover orchestration.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted observability (Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Jaeger, OpenTelemetry) when you want vendor-neutral data, custom retention, and to avoid per-GB ingestion billing; choose managed (CloudWatch, Cloud Operations, Azure Monitor) when you want zero-ops storage, alerting, and tight cloud integration.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed all-in-one observability suites (CloudWatch, Cloud Operations, Azure Monitor + Application Insights) bundle metrics, logs, traces, and alerting, so once you adopt them you may not need to run and scale your own Prometheus/Loki/Jaeger stack as the sharded fleet grows.

technologyChoices.tradeoff: Choose self-hosted/portable IaC and orchestration (Terraform, Kubernetes, Argo CD, Nomad) when you want multi-cloud portability and a single control plane; choose cloud-native tooling (CloudFormation/CDK, Auto Scaling, VMSS, MIGs) when you want deep provider integration and managed autoscaling without extra components.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed autoscaling groups (EC2 Auto Scaling, Managed Instance Groups, VM Scale Sets) and serverless platforms scale the stateless tier automatically, so the manual capacity provisioning behind the load-balancer step and bespoke scaling scripts become unnecessary.

followUps: How would you handle a master database failover safely when the slave is slightly behind?
How would you avoid the celebrity / hotspot problem when sharding by user_id?
How would you keep cache and database consistent on writes?
How would you do zero-downtime resharding when one shard fills up?
How would you handle session migration when promoting from sticky sessions to a stateless tier?
How would you choose between SQL and NoSQL for the user table at 100M users?
How would you handle multi-region writes when both DCs accept traffic?
How would you size the cache vs the database working set?
What metrics would page the on-call engineer first?

steps.title: 1. Single Server Setup
steps.description: Start with everything on one machine: the web app, the database, and any cache all run on a single server. DNS resolves www.mysite.com (web) and api.mysite.com (API) to the same IP. The server returns HTML to browsers and JSON to mobile apps.
steps.decisionPrompt: Define the smallest deployable slice: which responsibilities run on one box, what traffic can it serve, and what bottleneck should trigger the next split?

concepts.definition: Run web serving, application logic, data, and local cache on one machine.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the simplest deployable baseline and reveals the first bottlenecks clearly.

concepts.definition: A set of components that fail together because they share the same machine or dependency.
concepts.whyItMatters: One machine failing takes the whole application down.

deepDives.title: Request flow
points: User types www.mysite.com; the browser asks DNS for the IP.
DNS returns an IP like 15.125.23.214.
Browser opens HTTP to that IP; server returns HTML (or JSON for mobile clients).

deepDives.title: Two traffic sources
points: Web app: server-side language (Java/Python/...) for logic, HTML/JS for the presentation.
Mobile app: same HTTP transport, JSON is the common response format because it's compact and easy to parse.

bottlenecks.issue: Everything on one box — the server is a SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Split tiers and add redundancy in the steps that follow.

bottlenecks.issue: Single server caps both CPU and storage.
bottlenecks.mitigation: First separate the database; then scale each tier independently.

talkingPoints: DNS is typically a paid 3rd-party service, not something you run yourself.
It's fine to start here. Premature distribution is a common interview anti-pattern — make the bottleneck explicit before scaling it.

flows.name: Browser fetches HTML
flows.note: DNS lookup, then a direct HTTP GET to the single server.

flows.name: Mobile app fetches JSON
flows.note: Same path, different domain and response format.

view.caption: The user resolves the site through DNS and sends HTTP to a single web server that bundles web, database, and cache on one box.

steps.title: 2. Separate the Database
steps.description: As the user base grows, one server isn't enough. Pull the database off the web server into its own machine. Now the web tier and the data tier can be scaled independently, and one no longer crowds the other for CPU, memory, or disk.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the first persistence boundary: what data leaves the app server, how do app and database communicate, and what reliability improves or worsens?

concepts.definition: The point where durable data is separated from application process memory.
concepts.whyItMatters: App servers can restart without losing user records or business data.

concepts.definition: A bounded set of reusable database connections kept by the application.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects the database from too many concurrent client connections.

deepDives.title: SQL vs NoSQL
points: Relational (MySQL, Postgres, Oracle): mature, JOINs, transactions — the default for most apps.
NoSQL (DynamoDB, Cassandra, MongoDB, etc.): super-low latency, unstructured data, massive scale, or simple serialize/deserialize workloads.
Default to relational; reach for NoSQL when relational doesn't fit your access pattern, not because it's trendy.

deepDives.title: Why split the tier at all
points: Web tier and data tier have different resource profiles (CPU-bound vs IO-bound).
Independent scaling: add web servers without resizing the DB box, and vice versa.
Independent failure domains: a DB restart shouldn't take the web tier with it.

bottlenecks.issue: Web server is still a SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add a load balancer + multiple web servers (next step).

bottlenecks.issue: Single DB instance is still a SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add replication after the web tier is HA.

talkingPoints: This is the cheapest scalability win — almost no architectural complexity, big headroom unlock.
Treat 'which database' as a separate conversation from 'should the DB be its own tier'.

flows.name: Read with separate DB
flows.note: Web server forwards a read query to the database tier and returns the result.

view.caption: The user resolves DNS and hits the web server, which now reads and writes a separate standalone database tier.

steps.title: 3. Load Balancer + Multiple Web Servers
steps.description: One web server is a SPOF and caps throughput. Put a load balancer in front and run multiple web servers behind it. Clients hit only the LB's public IP; servers communicate over private IPs. Now a single server can fail without taking the site down, and capacity grows by adding servers to the pool.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design horizontal web scaling: how does the load balancer route requests, detect unhealthy servers, and preserve correctness when instances come and go?

concepts.definition: A routing component that spreads requests across healthy web servers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the web tier scale and tolerate individual server failures.

concepts.definition: Any web server can handle any request without relying on local user state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Load balancing and autoscaling work best when requests are interchangeable.

deepDives.title: Public vs private IPs
points: Only the load balancer has a public IP. Web servers live on private IPs, reachable only within the network.
This is a security improvement — backend servers aren't directly addressable from the internet.
The DNS A record points at the LB, not at any web server.

deepDives.title: Failover and capacity
points: If Server 1 goes offline, the LB routes everything to Server 2 and a new instance can be added to the pool.
If traffic grows past two servers, add more — the LB starts using them automatically.
Health checks decide who's in the pool; cheap /healthz checks process liveness.

bottlenecks.issue: LB itself can be a SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a managed/HA load balancer (ALB/NLB, GCLB) or pair LBs behind DNS.

bottlenecks.issue: Data tier still has one node.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Replication (next step).

bottlenecks.issue: Tempting to keep per-user state (like sessions) in web-server memory.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Keep web servers stateless from day one; the Stateless Web Tier step covers where session state should live (and why load-balancer session affinity hurts).

talkingPoints: Round-robin or least-connections is the default; avoid IP-hash unless you really need stickiness.
TLS terminates at the LB so backends speak plain HTTP internally — simpler ops, lower CPU on web nodes.

flows.name: Request through the load balancer
flows.note: Client hits the LB's public IP; LB picks a backend and forwards.

view.caption: The user reaches a load balancer that fans requests across web servers 1 and 2, both querying the shared database.

steps.title: 4. Database Replication
steps.description: One database is still a SPOF and caps throughput. Add master/slave replication: the master handles writes, slaves hold copies and serve reads. Most apps are heavily read-skewed, so this is a big win. On failure, a slave can be promoted to master.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the database replication model: which reads can go to replicas, how does failover happen, and where can stale reads hurt the product?

concepts.definition: Writes go to one primary database and are copied to read replicas.
concepts.whyItMatters: Read traffic can scale while writes keep one authoritative order.

concepts.definition: A user sees their own write on a later read.
concepts.whyItMatters: Replica lag can break this expectation unless recent reads go to the primary or use session routing.

deepDives.title: Replication model
points: Master handles INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE; slaves serve SELECT.
Slaves outnumber masters because read traffic dominates.
Replication is asynchronous by default — slaves can lag the master.

deepDives.title: Failure modes
points: If a slave dies: route its reads to the master or other slaves; bring a new slave online.
If the master dies: promote a slave; missing data may need recovery scripts if the slave wasn't fully caught up.
Multi-master and circular replication exist but are operationally heavier — out of scope until you really need them.

bottlenecks.issue: Master is still a write SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Sharding for write scale (later step); careful failover for availability.

bottlenecks.issue: Replica lag can return stale reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Read-your-writes from the master for sensitive paths; otherwise accept eventual consistency on reads.

talkingPoints: Performance, reliability, availability — replication addresses all three at the data tier.
Be explicit about which paths require read-after-write consistency. Most don't.

flows.name: Write to master, read from slave
flows.note: Writes go to the master; subsequent reads can be served from a slave once replication has propagated.

flows.name: Slave promotion on master failure
flows.note: If the master is lost, a slave is promoted and writes resume against it.

view.caption: The load balancer spreads traffic to web servers 1 and 2, which write to the master DB and read from slave DBs 1 and 2 that the master replicates to.

options.name: Single-leader, async replicas (default)
options.description: All writes go to one master while reads scale across asynchronous read replicas, and the master never waits on a replica to acknowledge a write.
pros: Writes go to one master and reads scale out across slaves - matches a read-heavy web app.
Async replication keeps writes fast since the master doesn't wait on slaves.
Simple, well-understood failover story (promote a slave).
cons: Replicas lag, so a user can read stale data right after their own write.
The master is a single write bottleneck and a failover gap exists during promotion.
Stale reads can hurt read-your-writes flows (profile edit, just-posted comment).

view.caption: Writes go to the master DB and reads to slaves 1 and 2 via the two web servers, with the master replicating asynchronously and never waiting on a slave.

options.name: Synchronous replication (read-your-writes safe)
options.description: The master waits for a replica to confirm each write before acknowledging, so every committed write is on a promotable replica and synced replicas serve fresh reads.
pros: The master waits for a replica to confirm, so a promoted replica has every acknowledged write - no data loss on failover.
Reads from a synced replica won't be stale, fixing read-your-writes.
Stronger durability guarantee for sensitive writes.
cons: Write latency now includes the slowest replica's round trip.
A slow or down replica can stall writes unless you fall back to async.
Throughput drops versus fully async replication.

view.caption: The web servers write to a sync-commit master that confirms each write on a replica before acking, so synced slaves 1 and 2 serve fresh reads.

options.name: Route recent writers to the leader
options.description: Replicas serve most reads, but a user who just wrote is routed to the master for their reads, keeping read-your-writes correct only where it matters.
pros: Send a user's read to the master only when they just wrote, replicas otherwise.
Keeps most reads off the master while preserving read-your-writes where it matters.
Tunable per route instead of one global consistency level.
cons: Application must track 'did this user just write' to pick the right source.
More routing logic in the web tier and easy to get subtly wrong.
Master still takes extra read load for recent-writer sessions.

view.caption: Both web servers write to the master DB and normally read from slave 1, but a user who just wrote is routed back to the master for their reads.

steps.title: 5. Add a Cache Tier
steps.description: Every page load triggers DB queries; the DB becomes the bottleneck. Add a cache tier in front of it. On a read, check the cache first; on a miss, query the DB and populate the cache. This is the read-through (cache-aside) pattern.
steps.decisionPrompt: Decide what to cache and at what granularity: estimate the hot read path, define miss behavior, and state the write invalidation rule.

concepts.definition: A cache or database key requested far more often than others.
concepts.whyItMatters: Hot keys can overload a single shard or cause cache stampedes.

concepts.definition: Many requests miss an expired cache entry at once and all hit the database.
concepts.whyItMatters: Stampedes can turn one cache expiry into a database outage.

deepDives.title: When to cache
points: Data that is read frequently but modified infrequently.
Volatile memory — never the source of truth. Persistent storage must hold the authoritative copy.
Set an expiration policy: too short reloads the DB needlessly; too long serves stale data.

deepDives.title: Consistency and failure
points: Cache and DB updates aren't atomic — invalidate or update the cache on write.
A single cache node is a SPOF. Use multiple cache servers across AZs.
Over-provision memory a bit so working-set growth doesn't immediately evict hot keys.

deepDives.title: Eviction
points: LRU is the most popular default.
LFU favours stable hot keys; FIFO is simpler but less effective.
Pick based on access pattern, not because the name sounds smart.

bottlenecks.issue: Cache stampede on a hot key's expiry.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Probabilistic early expiration or single-flight refresh.

bottlenecks.issue: Cache outage causes DB overload.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Multi-AZ cache; circuit breaker that rate-limits DB fallback.

bottlenecks.issue: Stale data after a write.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Update or invalidate on write; short TTL as a backstop.

talkingPoints: Mention strategies by name: cache-aside (default here), read-through, write-through, write-behind.
Quantify the hit rate trade-off from capacity numbers — don't hand-wave 'just add cache'.

flows.name: Read-through cache
flows.note: Cache hit returns immediately; cache miss falls through to the DB and backfills.

view.caption: The web servers look up the cache first and fall through to slave DBs 1 and 2 on a miss, while writes still go to the master DB.

options.name: Cache-aside (lazy load) (default)
options.description: The app reads the cache first and on a miss loads from a replica and populates the key, caching only requested data and deleting keys on write to invalidate.
pros: App checks cache, on miss reads the replica and populates - only requested data is cached.
Cache failure degrades to a slower DB read, not an outage.
Simple invalidation: delete the key on write.
cons: Every miss pays a DB read plus a cache write (cold-start stampede risk on hot keys).
Cache and DB can drift if invalidation is missed on a write.
First request for any key is always slow.

view.caption: The web servers read the cache and, on a miss, lazily load from a slave DB and populate the key, sending writes to the master DB.

options.name: Write-through cache
options.description: Every write updates the cache and the database together, so a written key is never stale and reads after a write are served fresh from cache.
pros: Writes update the cache and DB together, so the cache is never stale for written keys.
Reads after a write are guaranteed fresh from cache.
No separate invalidation step to forget.
cons: Every write pays the cache write even for data that's never read again.
Adds latency to the write path.
Still needs a TTL/eviction policy for cold keys never written through the app.

view.caption: The web servers use a write-through cache that is updated together with every master DB write, so a written key is served fresh and never stale.

steps.title: 6. Add a CDN for Static Content
steps.description: JS, CSS, images, and video are heavy and rarely change. Push them to a CDN — a network of geographically distributed cache servers — so users fetch static assets from a nearby edge instead of crossing oceans to the origin. The application server only serves dynamic responses.
steps.decisionPrompt: Separate static from dynamic delivery: which assets move to the CDN, how are cache keys and invalidation handled, and what remains on the origin?

concepts.definition: The backend storage or server a CDN fetches from on cache miss.
concepts.whyItMatters: The origin must be protected because cold misses and invalidations still reach it.
concepts.group: Add a CDN for Static Content

concepts.definition: Remove or bypass a cached object when a newer version should be served.
concepts.whyItMatters: Users should not keep receiving stale JS, CSS, or images after deployment.
concepts.group: Add a CDN for Static Content

deepDives.title: How a CDN serves a request
points: First request for image.png: edge has nothing cached, fetches from origin, stores with TTL, returns to the user.
Subsequent requests from any nearby user are served from the edge cache until TTL expires.
Edges are geo-distributed; users in LA hit the LA edge, users in Frankfurt hit the Frankfurt edge.

deepDives.title: Cost and freshness
points: CDNs are 3rd-party and bill on bytes — cache only assets that benefit from it (don't CDN-host rarely-used files).
TTL is a trade-off: too long serves stale, too short re-fetches from origin too often.
Invalidate via CDN API or use object versioning (image.png?v=2) to bypass cache cleanly.

deepDives.title: CDN failure
points: Plan for CDN outage: clients should be able to fall back to the origin.
Don't make the origin globally unreachable just because CDN is in front — that turns CDN into a SPOF.

bottlenecks.issue: CDN bills can scale unfavourably for unpopular assets.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Selectively serve cold assets from origin.

bottlenecks.issue: Stale assets after deploy.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Versioned URLs (asset hashing) so each deploy serves fresh URLs.

talkingPoints: Dynamic content caching exists but is a separate, more advanced topic — stay scoped to static here.
CDN dramatically reduces origin egress, which is often the biggest cloud bill line.

flows.name: Warm CDN hit
flows.note: Subsequent users get the asset from the edge directly.

flows.name: Cold CDN miss
flows.note: First user fetches the asset; the edge populates its cache from the origin.

view.caption: The user fetches static assets from the CDN edge (which pulls from the object-storage origin on a miss) and sends dynamic requests to the load balancer fronting the web and DB tiers.

steps.title: 7. Stateless Web Tier
steps.description: To scale the web tier horizontally — adding and removing servers freely — state must live outside the web servers. Move session data into a shared store (relational DB, Redis, or a NoSQL DB). Any web server can now handle any request, and autoscaling becomes safe.
steps.decisionPrompt: Remove hidden server affinity: where do sessions and uploaded state move so autoscaling, deploys, and failover do not lose user context?

concepts.definition: A small cookie the server sets (e.g. cookie=sess123) holding an opaque session id; the browser sends it on every request so the server can look up that user's session.
concepts.whyItMatters: It identifies the session but not where the session data lives — that choice (shared store, sticky server, or a self-contained token) is what makes the web tier stateless or not.

concepts.definition: A shared database or cache that stores session state outside web server memory.
concepts.whyItMatters: Users can move between web servers without losing login state.

concepts.definition: Load-balancer session affinity that pins each user to one web server so their in-memory session stays reachable.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids a shared session store but breaks autoscaling and graceful deploys: if that server is drained or dies, the user is logged out and load can't be rebalanced freely.

concepts.definition: Automatically add or remove server instances based on load signals.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is only safe when new instances can serve requests without manual warm state.

deepDives.title: Stateful vs stateless
points: Stateful: a user's session lives on a specific server, so all of that user's requests must be routed back to it (sticky sessions).
Stateless: any server can handle any request because state is fetched from a shared store.
Stateless is simpler, more robust, and a precondition for autoscaling.

deepDives.title: Where the state goes
points: Relational DB: works, but adds load to the master.
Redis/Memcached: fast, simple, common choice for sessions.
NoSQL (DynamoDB etc.): scales horizontally without admin effort — often the cleanest pick.

deepDives.title: Autoscaling unlocked
points: Servers come and go based on load — no warm-up of in-memory caches required.
Health-check failures recycle nodes safely because no user state is lost.
Deploys can do rolling restarts without breaking sessions.

bottlenecks.issue: Session store becomes a hot dependency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a managed, replicated store sized for peak login throughput.

bottlenecks.issue: Session token theft risk increases when sessions are easy to migrate.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Bind tokens to user agent / IP heuristics; rotate on privilege change.

talkingPoints: Sticky sessions are a smell at scale — call them out explicitly.
If you can't go stateless overnight, at least move *new* state out and let old state age out.

flows.name: Two requests hit different servers, same session
flows.note: Any server can serve the user because session data lives in the shared store.

view.caption: The load balancer now spreads dynamic traffic across four web servers that share session state in an external Redis/NoSQL session store, plus the cache and master DB.

options.name: External session store (default)
options.description: Session data lives in a shared store such as Redis that every web server reads, so any server can handle any user's request and a dying server drops no session.
pros: Sessions live in a shared store (Redis/NoSQL), so any web server can serve any user - autoscaling and deploys don't drop context.
A web server can die mid-session with no user-visible logout.
Server-side sessions can be revoked instantly.
cons: Adds a network round trip to the session store on most requests.
The session store becomes a shared dependency to scale and keep available.
Slightly more infrastructure than in-process sessions.

view.caption: All four web servers read and write a shared external session store, so any server can serve any user and a lost server drops no session.

options.name: Sticky sessions (load-balancer affinity)
options.description: Sessions stay in each web server's memory and the load balancer pins each user to one server, so session access is in-process with no shared store.
pros: Keep sessions in web-server memory and pin each user to one server - no session store needed.
Fastest session access (in-process, no network hop).
Minimal change to a single-server app.
cons: If that server dies or is drained on deploy, the user is logged out - defeats stateless autoscaling.
Load can skew because a user can't be freely rebalanced.
Scaling down or rolling deploys disrupt active sessions.

view.caption: The load balancer pins each user to web server 1 or 2, whose sessions live in that server's own memory with no shared store.

options.name: Stateless tokens (signed JWT in the client)
options.description: The session is carried in a signed token the client sends on every request, which any server validates locally, holding no server-side session state at all.
pros: The session lives in a signed token the client sends each request - no server-side session lookup at all.
Any server validates the signature locally, so there's zero shared session state.
Scales effortlessly and survives any server loss.
cons: Revoking a token before expiry needs a blocklist, partly reintroducing shared state.
Tokens can grow large and ride on every request.
Secret/key rotation and token theft must be handled carefully.

view.caption: The user carries a signed JWT that the load balancer routes to web server 1 or 2, each validating it locally with no server-side session state.

steps.title: 8. Multiple Data Centers
steps.description: International users want low latency and tolerance for regional failures. Run in two (or more) data centers, route users via geoDNS to the nearest one, and replicate data between regions. On a DC outage, geoDNS redirects 100% of traffic to the healthy DC.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the multi-data-center topology: how are users routed, what data is replicated, and what happens during regional outage or split-brain risk?

concepts.definition: DNS routing that directs users to a region based on geography or health.
concepts.whyItMatters: Users reach a nearby or healthy data center without changing the application URL.

concepts.definition: Shift traffic away from a failed data center to another region.
concepts.whyItMatters: Availability depends on both routing and data being ready in the failover region.

deepDives.title: Traffic redirection
points: GeoDNS resolves the same domain to different IPs based on user location.
Most days, traffic splits x% / (100-x)% by region.
On outage, redirect 100% to the healthy region — geoDNS is the steering layer.

deepDives.title: Data synchronization
points: Replicate data across regions — otherwise the failover destination won't have the data.
Async cross-region replication is the common pattern (see Netflix's active/active write-up).
Be honest about RPO/RTO — async replication means some lag.

deepDives.title: Test and deploy
points: Test from each region — latency from each edge to each DC.
Automated deploys must keep services consistent across all DCs.
Run failover drills regularly. The first time you fail over should not be in production at 3am.

bottlenecks.issue: Cross-region writes are slow and conflict-prone.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Either active/passive (writes only in primary) or partition the keyspace by region.

bottlenecks.issue: GeoDNS caching delays failover.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Short TTLs on the geoDNS records; supplement with client-side fallback logic.

talkingPoints: Active/active vs active/passive is a major call — name it explicitly.
Storage replication and traffic routing are independent levers — design them together but keep them conceptually separate.

flows.name: Normal operation — geo-routed traffic
flows.note: GeoDNS picks the nearest healthy DC for each user.

messages.text: routes EU users to the closer region

flows.name: DC outage — failover
flows.note: US-West is down; geoDNS routes all traffic to US-East.

view.caption: GeoDNS routes the user to the nearest of two full regional stacks (US-East and US-West), each with its own LB, web, cache, and DB, replicating their databases cross-region.

options.name: Active-active, async cross-region replication (default)
options.description: Both regions serve reads and writes locally and replicate to each other asynchronously, with GeoDNS shedding a failed region's traffic to the survivor.
pros: Both regions serve reads and writes locally, so users get low latency and capacity doubles.
A regional outage just sheds that region's traffic to the other via GeoDNS.
No idle standby capacity sitting unused.
cons: Concurrent writes in two regions can conflict - needs conflict resolution or per-key region ownership.
Async replication means cross-region reads can be stale.
Hardest topology to get correct; split-brain risk during partitions.

view.caption: Both US-East and US-West stacks serve reads and writes locally and replicate asynchronously, with GeoDNS able to shift a failed region's traffic to the survivor.

options.name: Active-passive (warm standby + failover)
options.description: One region takes all writes while the other is a warm read replica promoted only on failover, so there is a single write region and no cross-region write conflicts.
pros: One region takes all writes; the other is a read replica promoted only on failover - no write-conflict problem.
Simpler correctness: a single write region means one source of truth.
Clear DR story with a known RPO/RTO.
cons: The standby's capacity is mostly idle until failover.
Users far from the active region pay cross-region write latency.
Failover/promotion is a risky, occasionally-exercised operation.

view.caption: GeoDNS sends traffic to the active US-East region whose DB takes all writes, while US-West is a warm standby read replica fed by cross-region replication.

steps.title: 9. Message Queue for Async Work
steps.description: Some operations are slow (image processing, sending email, video transcoding) and don't belong on the request path. Introduce a message queue: web servers publish jobs and return immediately; pools of workers consume independently. Producers and consumers scale separately based on queue depth.
steps.decisionPrompt: Move slow work off the request path: choose the queue boundary, define retry and idempotency behavior, and decide what the user sees while work is pending.

concepts.definition: Work accepted during a request but completed later by background workers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Slow tasks like image processing should not hold open user requests.

concepts.definition: A worker that can process the same job more than once without corrupting results.
concepts.whyItMatters: Queues retry jobs, so duplicate execution must be safe.

deepDives.title: Producer / consumer model
points: Producers (web servers) publish messages without waiting for them to be processed.
Consumers (workers) subscribe and process at their own pace.
If consumers are down, messages buffer in the queue and get processed when consumers return.

deepDives.title: Scaling independently
points: Queue depth growing: add more workers.
Queue depth at zero most of the time: scale workers down.
Producer load and consumer load no longer need to match in real time.

deepDives.title: Use cases
points: Photo customization (cropping, sharpening, blurring) — slow per item, perfectly parallel.
Email / push notifications.
Analytics fan-out — the hot path emits events; downstream consumers handle aggregation.

bottlenecks.issue: Queue backpressure can stall producers.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Async publish with bounded local buffer; shed non-critical events under pressure.

bottlenecks.issue: Workers fail mid-job, losing progress.
bottlenecks.mitigation: At-least-once delivery + idempotent workers; dead-letter queues for poison messages.

bottlenecks.issue: Ordering guarantees vary by queue.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Pick a queue (Kafka, SQS FIFO, RabbitMQ) whose ordering and delivery semantics match the use case.

talkingPoints: Logging, metrics, and automation arrive around the same time as the queue — once the system is too big to operate by intuition.
Don't put the synchronous hot path behind the queue — only work that the user is happy to wait on (or doesn't even know is happening).

flows.name: Photo upload — async processing
flows.note: Web server publishes the job and responds immediately; a worker picks it up and processes the image.

view.caption: The web servers enqueue slow jobs on a message queue that dispatches to workers 1, 2, and 3, which write results back to the database while reads still use the cache.

steps.title: 10. Shard the Database (and Add Tooling)
steps.description: Data keeps growing; a single master, even with replicas, eventually hits hardware limits. Shard horizontally — split data across multiple databases using a sharding key (e.g. user_id % N). At the same time, formalize logging, metrics, and automation: at this scale, operating by intuition stops working.
steps.decisionPrompt: Pick a database partitioning strategy: choose the shard key, explain routing and resharding, and identify the queries that become harder.

concepts.definition: One partition of a larger logical database.
concepts.whyItMatters: Shards let storage and write throughput grow horizontally.

concepts.definition: Move data between shards when the current partitioning no longer fits load or size.
concepts.whyItMatters: A sharding plan is incomplete if it cannot evolve.

deepDives.title: Choosing a sharding key
points: The key determines how rows distribute — pick one that spreads load evenly.
user_id is a common choice; the modulo (or a hash) routes to a shard.
Every query needs the sharding key to avoid cross-shard scatter-gather.

deepDives.title: Resharding and consistent hashing
points: Adding shards naively rehashes everything — disastrous in production.
Consistent hashing minimizes data movement when shard count changes.
Resharding is rare but planned-for; tooling matters more than the algorithm.

deepDives.title: Celebrity / hotspot problem
points: If a few keys (celebrities) are read or written far more than the rest, their shard melts.
Mitigations: give each hot key its own shard, or split a celebrity across multiple replicas behind a fan-out layer.
Detect early — top-N key dashboards on every shard.

deepDives.title: Joins and de-normalization
points: Cross-shard joins are slow and brittle — usually not feasible.
De-normalize so each query can be answered from a single shard.
Move clearly non-relational workloads (e.g. user feed timelines) to a NoSQL store sized for them.

deepDives.title: Logging, metrics, automation
points: Logging: aggregate to a central log service so engineers don't SSH for grep.
Metrics: host (CPU/mem/disk), tier (DB latency, cache hit rate), and business (DAU, retention, revenue).
Automation: CI/CD, automated tests, automated deploys — at this scale, manual ops are the bottleneck.

bottlenecks.issue: Hotspot shards (celebrity problem).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-key dedicated shards; further partition; cache aggressively.

bottlenecks.issue: Cross-shard transactions.
bottlenecks.mitigation: De-normalize; avoid; use sagas or at-least-once + idempotency where needed.

bottlenecks.issue: Resharding downtime.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Online resharding via dual-write + backfill + cutover; consistent hashing to minimize moved keys.

talkingPoints: Summary of the journey: keep web tier stateless, build redundancy at every tier, cache aggressively, support multiple DCs, host static on CDN, shard the data tier, split into services, monitor + automate.
Beyond millions of users you keep iterating — decompose into services, optimize hot paths, repeat. The toolbox is the same.

flows.name: Sharded write — routed by user_id
flows.note: Shard router hashes the user_id and forwards to the matching shard.

flows.name: Sharded read with cache
flows.note: Cache absorbs hot reads; misses go through the router to the right shard.

view.caption: The web servers route queries through a user_id % N shard router across shards 0-3, also reading a NoSQL store and emitting to logs/metrics/automation tooling.

options.name: Hash sharding (user_id % N) (default)
options.description: A stateless router hashes the shard key to pick a shard, spreading rows evenly so a single-user query hits exactly one shard.
pros: Hashing the shard key spreads rows evenly, avoiding hotspots.
Routing is a cheap, stateless computation in the shard router.
Single-user queries hit exactly one shard.
cons: Changing N (resharding) remaps most rows - a painful migration.
Range/scan queries (e.g. 'all users created last week') must fan out to every shard.
Cross-shard joins and transactions become application concerns.

view.caption: A stateless router hashes user_id % N to send each single-user query to exactly one of shards 0 through 3, spreading rows evenly.

options.name: Range sharding (key ranges per shard)
options.description: Each shard owns a contiguous key range, so rows stay ordered and range or recent-data scans hit a single shard, with a range map the router consults.
pros: Rows stay ordered, so range scans and 'recent' queries hit one shard.
Splitting a hot range into two shards is a localized operation.
Natural fit for time-ordered or alphabetical access patterns.
cons: Sequential keys (auto-increment ids, timestamps) hotspot the newest range.
Uneven key distribution needs ongoing range re-splitting to rebalance.
Requires a range map the router must keep current.

view.caption: The router consults a range map to send keys to the shard owning that range (a-h, h-p, and so on), keeping rows ordered for range scans.

options.name: Directory-based (lookup table) sharding
options.description: A directory table maps each key or tenant to its shard, decoupling placement from the key value so keys can be moved by updating the directory.
pros: A directory maps each key (or tenant) to its shard, decoupling placement from the key value.
Resharding moves chosen keys without a global remap - just update the directory.
Lets you co-locate a tenant's data or rebalance hotspots deliberately.
cons: The directory is an extra lookup on the hot path and a potential bottleneck/SPOF.
It must be cached and kept consistent with actual data placement.
Adds operational surface versus a stateless hash function.

view.caption: The router looks each key up in a lookup directory that maps it to a shard, so placement is decoupled from the key and rows can be relocated.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end scaled web application architecture: CDN for static assets, load-balanced stateless web tier, shared session/cache layers, replicated and sharded databases, queues for asynchronous work, workers, object storage, and operational tooling.

options.name: Full scaled architecture
options.description: The complete topology with CDN, stateless web tier, cache, external sessions, a sharded primary store plus NoSQL, queues with workers, object storage, and monitoring.
pros: Separates static, dynamic, data, and async workloads
Stateless web tier supports autoscaling and deploys
Database sharding and queues extend scale beyond one server
Monitoring and automation are explicit
cons: Many operational components for a simple product
Cache/session/database consistency must be managed
Sharding makes queries and migrations more complex

view.caption: Users reach GeoDNS and the CDN, then the load balancer and stateless web tier backed by the cache, session store, sharded DB with replicas, object store, and a message queue feeding async workers to blob storage, all reporting to metrics/logs.

options.name: Single-region replicated architecture
options.description: One region with a CDN, stateless web tier, cache, external sessions, a single primary database with a read replica, and a queue with workers, before any sharding.
pros: Good intermediate target before sharding
Lower operational complexity
Covers common availability and read-scale needs
cons: One primary database remains the write bottleneck
Region outage still hurts availability
Async and storage scaling are more limited

view.caption: Within one region, users hit the CDN and load balancer, the stateless web servers use the cache and session store, write to the primary DB and read from its replica, and offload work through the message queue to workers.

Users hit GeoDNS and the CDN, then the load balancer and stateless web tier, which use the cache and session store, route sharded queries through the shard router, read the object store, and offload slow work to the queue and async workers, all feeding metrics/logs.
